The strongest teacher solution keeps the plotting steps explicit and uses a small
table that students can inspect directly before any figure is created.

## Suggested approach

1. Load and sort the data first so the line plot has a clear order on the x-axis.
2. Use separate figures for the line, scatter, and bar plots so each plot type stays
   easy to read.
3. Add titles and axis labels every time so students build good habits immediately.
4. Save the final line plot before displaying it so the output file is guaranteed to
   contain the finished figure.

## Minimal reference implementation

```python
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.read_csv(
    "assets/campus_store_plotting_data.csv",
    parse_dates=["date"],
).sort_values("date")
print(sales.head())

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(sales["date"], sales["revenue"], marker="o")
plt.title("Revenue over time")
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.scatter(sales["orders"], sales["revenue"])
plt.title("Orders versus revenue")
plt.xlabel("Orders")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

avg_revenue_by_channel = sales.groupby("channel")["revenue"].mean()
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.bar(avg_revenue_by_channel.index, avg_revenue_by_channel.values)
plt.title("Average revenue by channel")
plt.xlabel("Channel")
plt.ylabel("Average revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(sales["date"], sales["revenue"], marker="o")
plt.title("Revenue over time")
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("revenue_trend.png")
plt.show()
```

## What students should notice

- line plots work best when the x-axis has an order,
- scatter plots help compare two numeric variables without implying a sequence,
- bar plots are easier to build after a grouped Pandas summary,
- plot titles and labels are part of the analysis, not just presentation polish,
- the saved figure comes from the same reproducible code as the on-screen figure.

## Teaching emphasis

Keep the focus on choosing the plot type deliberately and making each plot readable.
The goal of this slice is not advanced styling. It is the first stable bridge from
tables and arrays into clear visual communication.
